sequences.name: Welcome Sequence
sequences.description: Triggered on Practice Partnership Assessment completion or lead magnet download. 7 emails over 14 days. Moves from assessment completion to active evaluation.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** The number that made us build SGA Dental Partners.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Your Practice Partnership Assessment results are ready. You can review your full report here:

View My Assessment Results

Take a few minutes with it. The scoring is straightforward, and the recommendations are specific to where your practice sits right now -- not generic advice from a consulting binder that collects dust on a shelf.

Now, a quick introduction.

My name is sender name, and I am writing to you from SGA Dental Partners. We are not a brokerage. We are not a consulting firm. We are a dental partnership platform backed by the Thurston Group -- a private equity firm that has spent 38 years and 8 healthcare platforms learning how to do this right.

Why am I telling you that?

Because there is a number that haunts this industry: seven out of ten dentists who sell to a DSO end up frustrated, annoyed, or outright regretful.

That is not our number. That is what the Dentist Advisors podcast found after talking to hundreds of practice owners who took the deal, cashed the check, and woke up six months later wondering what they had done.

We built SGA because we knew that number -- and we refused to add to it.

Over the next few emails, I am going to share what makes this platform different. Not marketing claims. Names, numbers, and stories from dentists who sound a lot like you.

If at any point you want to skip ahead and have a direct conversation, you can schedule one here:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

No brokers. No NDAs at this stage. Just a conversation between professionals.

Talk soon,

Sender name
SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- Your assessment results do not expire, but the market context around them shifts quarterly. The analysis is most useful while the data is fresh.

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** And nobody talks about it at the dental society dinner.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

There is a question that practice owners ask themselves late at night, usually after the last staff email is answered and the house is finally quiet.

"How much longer can I keep doing this?"

Not the dentistry. The dentistry is why you got into this. The dentistry is the part that still makes sense.

It is everything else.

You are the dentist, the HR department, the IT guy, and the therapist for your whole staff. You did not sign up for any of that. But here you are -- spending Sunday nights fixing the schedule because your office manager gave notice, arguing with insurance companies over claims that should have been paid six weeks ago, and watching younger dentists adopt digital workflows you have never had time to learn.

One practice owner we spoke to put it this way: "I traded my passion for a payroll spreadsheet."

Another said: "Stressed out and burned out. How could we deal with owning two dental practices?"

Here is what nobody mentions at the dental society dinner: this feeling is not a personal failure. It is a structural one. The business of dentistry has outgrown what a single human being can manage, no matter how smart or how hard they work.

The dentists who figure this out early do not work harder. They change the structure.

That is what tomorrow's email is about -- the people who decided to change it, and the 38-year track record behind them.

Read My Assessment Results Again

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- If you are reading this and nodding, you already know the business side is not sustainable. The question is what you do about it. That is the most common thing we hear in first conversations with practice owners. No judgment here. Just understanding.

emails.subject: Email 3
emails.preheader: ** This is not a first-time fund experimenting with dental.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

The number one fear dentists have about DSO partnerships is not the valuation. It is not the management fee. It is not even the autonomy question -- though that is a close second.

It is private equity.

"They load up on debt, squeeze margins, and flip the company in 3-5 years. Then I'm stuck with a new owner who doesn't care about me."

We have heard this objection a hundred times. And honestly, in most cases, dentists are right to be skeptical. The PE-backed DSO track record is, to put it politely, uneven.

So let me tell you about the Thurston Group.

Patrick J. Haynes III founded the firm in 1988. That is 38 years ago. While most PE funds operate on 5-7 year cycles, Thurston has been building healthcare platforms for nearly four decades.

The numbers:

- $4 billion+ returned to partners across the firm's history
- 8 dental and healthcare platforms built: Smile Doctors, US Endodontics Partners, US Oral Surgery Management, ARC Health, US Orthopaedic Partners, and the dental platforms now unified under SGA
- A $350 million credit facility secured for SGA's continued growth

But the number that matters most is this: Thurston did not get it right the first time. They built, learned, and rebuilt. South Georgia Dental Management taught them community dentistry operations. Gen4 Dental Partners taught them that doctor autonomy could not just be promised -- it had to be structural. MODIS Dental Partners taught them that education was the competitive moat, not purchasing power.

SGA is the synthesis of all three. Thirty-eight years of iteration.

These are not projections. That is history. And the partners from prior Thurston platforms are not anonymous case studies -- they are people you can call.

Tomorrow, I am going to tell you about the part of SGA that no competitor can replicate: our education platform. It starts with a name you may already know.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- If "private equity" makes your stomach turn, you are in good company. That is exactly why we lead with the track record, not the pitch deck. The evidence speaks louder than any brochure.

emails.subject: Email 4
emails.preheader: ** This is not a CE stipend. It is a clinical culture.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Every DSO in America offers a continuing education stipend. Usually $1,500 to $3,000 a year. Enough to attend one generic seminar at a hotel conference room, eat a mediocre lunch, and come home with a binder of slides you will never open again.

That is not what we do.

SGA was built around education as the soul of the platform, not a line item on the benefits page. Here is what that actually means:

The Pikos Institute. Dr. Michael A. Pikos -- a pioneer in implantology and one of the most respected educators in global dentistry -- is a founding partner of our MODIS specialty vertical. His institute provides world-class, hands-on implantology training. Not webinars. Not slide decks. Hands-on surgical technique with the person who wrote the textbook.

The 4Front Symposium. Our annual gathering of partner clinicians. This is where the goose bumps happen. Senior clinicians brainstorming together, elevating ideas collectively. As one partner described it: "They're like, 'I've never thought about it like that.' Now you got me."

The Individual Growth Engine. Every partner gets paired with a clinical mentor -- not a corporate trainer reading a script. A practicing dentist who has been where you are and can show you what they see. Dr. Matt Cavendish was paired with Dr. Bryan Shanahan through this program. More on his story in my next email.

Dentists teaching dentists. That is the phrase you will hear more than any other inside SGA. Not consultants teaching dentists. Not MBAs teaching dentists. Clinicians sharing what works, in the operatory, with someone who understands the craft.

Here is why this matters to you specifically:

You told us in your assessment that personalization based on assessment response about clinical growth / technology / peer isolation. The education platform is designed to solve exactly that -- not in theory, but in practice, with people who have already walked the path.

No competitor has the Pikos Institute. No competitor has the 4Front Symposium. No competitor has 100+ clinicians who actively teach each other.

This is not something money buys. It is something you build over years. And we have built it.

Learn More About the Pikos Institute

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- If you have ever attended a Pikos course, you already know what I am describing. If you have not, this alone is worth a conversation.

emails.subject: Email 5
emails.preheader: ** The transformation nobody talks about -- what happens AFTER you partner.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Dr. Matt Cavendish had been practicing for 20 years in Phoenix. By every external measure, he was successful. Good revenue. Good reputation. Solid patient base.

But privately, he was in a different place.

"Maybe was in a spot of complacency, not being challenged as much, just getting through the day and not enjoying it as much."

Sound familiar?

Twenty years in, the spark was fading. He was spending his time on employee benefits, wifi routers, Facebook ads -- "a hundred other things that have little to do with dental excellence."

When staff issues came up: "I was just trying to figure out by myself what I needed to do. It was very difficult."

Then he joined Gen4 (now part of SGA). And something unexpected happened.

He got paired with a mentor -- Dr. Bryan Shanahan -- through the Individual Growth Engine. He observed his first all-on-four procedure. He learned surgical techniques, treatment planning approaches, and lab relationships he never would have accessed in solo practice.

"Just to pick his brain on his surgical technique, treatment planning, and the way he works with his lab -- that's something I would have never had an opportunity to do if I were still in a solo practice."

Here are his numbers since partnering:

- Revenues trailing twelve months: up 16.5%
- EBITA: beating plan by 2.3 points, beating prior year by $45,266
- Total payroll expenses: down
- Staff retention: 100%
- Cost of dental supplies: beating plan by 6.4 points

But the number Dr. Cavendish talks about most is not on a spreadsheet.

"When you've been in dentistry for 20 years like I have, sometimes it's not as fun as it used to be. So to be able to do a complex case like this, with somebody who is also working at a very high skill level -- it is fun."

It is fun again.

That is what nobody tells you about partnering with SGA. Not the financial relief -- every DSO promises that. Not the operational support -- that is table stakes. The thing nobody tells you is that you might actually enjoy dentistry again.

Dr. Cavendish has one piece of advice for dentists considering the same decision:

"Don't push back. There's no downside to having a mentor."

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Dr. Cavendish is a real person at a real practice. If you want to hear it from him directly, we can arrange that conversation. Just ask.

emails.subject: Email 6
emails.preheader: ** No other DSO offers this structural breadth.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Most DSOs are built for one type of practice. General dentistry platforms acquire general practices. Specialty platforms acquire specialists. And when they encounter a practice that does not fit their mold, they either force it to fit or walk away.

SGA is built differently. We operate three distinct verticals under one platform, each purpose-built for a different kind of practice:

1. Community Dentistry

Hometown general practices serving the families and communities that built them. Our original SGA network spans 17+ practice groups across Georgia, Mississippi, Alabama, Louisiana, and Tennessee. These are practices where the dentist coaches Little League and the hygienist's kids go to the same school as the patients. The operational support is centralized. The community identity is sacred.

2. Collaborative General & Specialty Practice

Multi-location groups and specialty practices that thrive on peer collaboration. Our Gen4 model spans practices across Arizona, California, Nevada, Minnesota, Michigan, Texas, and beyond. The difference here is the culture: dentists teaching dentists, the 4Front Symposium, the Individual Growth Engine. These are practices where clinical growth is not optional -- it is the expectation.

3. Advanced Implantology

Specialty and implant-focused practices anchored by the Pikos Institute and innovators like Dr. Grant Olson (138,000 YouTube subscribers from Springfield, Missouri). Our MODIS vertical serves periodontists, oral surgeons, and implantologists who need capital investment, marketing infrastructure, and recruiting support to compete at the level their clinical skills deserve.

Here is why three verticals matter to you:

Your practice does not have to be one thing to fit inside SGA. If you are a general practitioner who wants to develop implant skills, the Pikos Institute pathway exists. If you have a multi-specialty group, the collaborative model supports every discipline. If you run a hometown family practice and want to keep it exactly that -- we have been doing precisely that in South Georgia for over a decade.

No other DSO offers this range. And that is not a marketing claim -- it is a structural reality you can verify by looking at our partner list.

See Our Partner Practices

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Not sure which vertical fits your practice? That is exactly what the confidential conversation is for. We do not start with a pitch. We start with a fit evaluation.

emails.subject: Email 7
emails.preheader: ** This is the email where I ask. Directly.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Over the past two weeks I have shared:

- The number that haunts this industry (7 out of 10 dentists regret their DSO sale) and why SGA was built to be the exception
- The Thurston Group's 38-year, $4B+ track record across 8 dental and healthcare platforms
- An education platform anchored by the Pikos Institute, the 4Front Symposium, and the Individual Growth Engine -- things no competitor can replicate
- Dr. Matt Cavendish's transformation from complacent to rejuvenated, with revenues up 16.5% and staff retention at 100%
- Three clinical verticals that mean your practice does not have to change to fit our model

I have not asked you for anything. Until now.

Here is what I am asking:

Schedule a confidential conversation with our partnership team.

Not a sales call. Not a pitch meeting. A 30-minute conversation between professionals about what a partnership could look like for your specific practice. You will talk to someone who has sat in your chair -- a dentist or a dental-industry veteran who understands what you are weighing.

Here is what one partner said about the experience:

"SGA supports us and manages things behind the scenes. Our partnership allows us to focus on changing lives."

That is Dr. Mitchel Godat, Periodontal Associates of Memphis. He kept his name. He kept his team. He kept his practice exactly the way he built it. What changed was everything behind the scenes -- and the weight he no longer carries alone.

If you are ready to explore what that looks like for you:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

If you are not ready yet, that is fine. I will continue to share industry insights, partner stories, and market data over the coming weeks. The door does not close.

But if there is a voice in the back of your mind -- the one that surfaces at 2 AM, the one that wonders whether there is a way to keep what you built and still get relief -- this is the conversation that answers it.

No commitments. No brokers. No NDAs at this stage.

Just a direct conversation about what is possible.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name
SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- If you want to talk to a current partner before you talk to us, we can arrange that too. Dr. Cavendish, Dr. Godat, and others have offered to speak directly with prospective partners. Just reply to this email and ask.

sequences.name: Nurture Sequence
sequences.description: Triggered after Welcome Sequence completes without booking. 7 emails over 28 days. Builds trust through education and industry intelligence.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** The data your broker probably has not shown you.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

A quick market update you will not get from a broker or a trade magazine headline.

The dental acquisition market in 2026 is moving faster than most practice owners realize. Here is what the numbers show:

The buyer landscape is consolidating. There are over 1,000 IDSOs operating today. But fewer than 100 are considered qualified bidders by industry experts. That means the vast majority of offers dentists receive come from organizations without the capital, infrastructure, or track record to deliver on their promises.

Multiples are strong -- for now. Mid-market practices (1-3 doctors, $1M-$3M revenue) are trading at 7-9x EBITDA. Group practices with 4+ doctors are commanding 9-12x. These are historically elevated numbers driven by PE fund deployment cycles and competition for quality targets. Whether this holds depends on interest rates, market saturation, and the performance of the wave of acquisitions made in 2022-2024.

The talent crisis is accelerating the math. Over 76% of practices find hygienist recruiting "extremely challenging." When a solo practice cannot fill a hygiene chair, revenue drops 15-20%. When a DSO-affiliated practice has a dedicated recruiting team and a benefits package that includes 401(k), Cigna health insurance, and real PTO, the talent flows toward the platform. This dynamic favors practices with infrastructure -- whether that infrastructure is homegrown or partnered.

What this means for you:

If you are a practice owner doing $1M+ in revenue, you are a target. Every broker, every DSO, and every private equity-backed platform in the country is looking at practices like yours. The question is not whether you will be approached. The question is whether you will be approached by the right people with the right structure -- or by the wrong ones with a glossy pitch deck and a 3-year exit horizon.

SGA is backed by the Thurston Group -- 38 years, $4B+ returned, 8 dental and healthcare platforms built. We are not a first-time fund experimenting with your career.

I will share more market intelligence in the coming weeks. If you want to discuss how these trends apply to your specific practice:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

Multiples are strong -- for now. Mid-market practices (1-3 doctors, $1M-$3M revenue) are trading at 7-9x EBITDA. Group practices with 4+ doctors are commanding 9-12x. These are historically elevated numbers driven by PE fund deployment cycles and competition for quality targets. Whether this holds depends on interest rates, market saturation, and the performance of the wave of acquisitions made in 2022-2024.

The talent crisis is accelerating the math. Over 76% of practices find hygienist recruiting "extremely challenging." When a solo practice cannot fill a hygiene chair, revenue drops 15-20%. When a DSO-affiliated practice has a dedicated recruiting team and a benefits package that includes 401(k), Cigna health insurance, and real PTO, the talent flows toward the platform. This dynamic favors practices with infrastructure -- whether that infrastructure is homegrown or partnered.

What this means for you:

If you are a practice owner doing $1M+ in revenue, you are a target. Every broker, every DSO, and every private equity-backed platform in the country is looking at practices like yours. The question is not whether you will be approached. The question is whether you will be approached by the right people with the right structure -- or by the wrong ones with a glossy pitch deck and a 3-year exit horizon.

SGA is backed by the Thurston Group -- 38 years, $4B+ returned, 8 dental and healthcare platforms built. We are not a first-time fund experimenting with your career.

I will share more market intelligence in the coming weeks. If you want to discuss how these trends apply to your specific practice:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** The three mistakes that turn a partnership into a prison.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

"Seven or eight out of 10 are pretty bugged or annoyed or frustrated or regretful. It's not what they thought."

That is what the hosts of the Dentist Advisors podcast found after speaking with hundreds of dentists who sold to DSOs. Not a survey with a 5% response rate. Hundreds of real conversations with real dentists.

And it gets worse.

One dentist they spoke to had $10 million in cash from the sale. Ten million dollars. And he said:

"I wish I would have never done this."

So what goes wrong? After studying the patterns across every available source -- Dentaltown, White Coat Investor, Student Doctor Network, Glassdoor reviews, industry podcasts -- three structural failures account for most of the regret:

Failure 1: Promised support never materialized.

"I didn't get the support I thought. They just like took control or sort of control, but I'm still like running it."

The DSO promised to handle billing, HR, compliance. In practice, they charged a management fee and handed the dentist a login to new software. The administrative burden did not decrease. It just moved from QuickBooks to a different platform with a different set of problems.

Failure 2: Equity and financial terms were worse than expected.

"The money, they have a clawback or they had some type of provision that they didn't know about, or the equity did not turn out to be what they thought."

The multiple looked good on paper. But the DSO manipulated the math -- management fees, carried notes, earnout structures with unreachable targets. The headline number and the actual check were very different.

Failure 3: Autonomy eroded gradually.

"When you sell, you are no longer the employer -- you are the employee."

Nobody said "you can't use that lab" on day one. Instead, it was a slow drift. New supply contracts. Scheduling templates. Production expectations. Suggested treatment protocols that became mandatory. By month 18, the practice owner looked around and realized they were running someone else's practice with their name on the door.

What the other 3 out of 10 did differently:

They chose partners with verifiable track records -- not pitch decks. They talked to existing partners directly, not just the business development team. They verified that clinical autonomy was contractual, not just conversational. And they aligned with organizations where the equity model had been tested across multiple platforms and hold periods.

That is the model we built at SGA. Not because we are smarter than other DSOs. Because we studied why they fail, and we refused to repeat the pattern.

If you want to understand specifically how SGA's structure addresses each of these three failure modes, I am happy to walk through it:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- This is not an attack on other DSOs. There are good operators in this market. But the data says most are not -- and you deserve to know the difference before you sign anything.

emails.subject: Email 3
emails.preheader: ** Your materials. Your lab. Your schedule. Your treatment plans. In writing.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Every DSO in America claims clinical autonomy. It has become marketing wallpaper -- a phrase so overused that it has lost all meaning.

So let me be specific about what SGA means when we say it. And more importantly, let me tell you what it looks like when other organizations do not mean it.

What clinical autonomy sounds like at a typical DSO:

"You want to be in control of your materials, your labs, your assistants." -- A dentist on Student Doctor Network, describing what they expected.

"Decisions are being made about what your production is supposed to be by people wearing suits in a boardroom." -- What they got instead.

"Focus too much on profit hidden behind 'crown days' or 'Invisalign days' pushing doctors to focus on higher cost treatment." -- A Heartland Dental employee, Glassdoor review.

The pattern is consistent. Autonomy is promised in the recruitment conversation and eroded after the deal closes. Not through a single dramatic mandate, but through a thousand small changes: new preferred labs, standardized supply lists, scheduling templates that prioritize volume, and "clinical guidelines" that look suspiciously like production targets.

What clinical autonomy actually means at SGA:

It is written into the partnership agreement. Specifically:

- You choose your materials. Not a corporate supply list. Your materials.
- You choose your lab. The lab relationships you have built over years of practice stay intact.
- You choose your schedule. SGA does not dictate patient volume or appointment length.
- You choose your treatment plans. Clinical decisions are made by clinicians, not by MBAs reviewing your production reports.

Our CCO, Dr. Mitch Ellingson, is a practicing dentist who has personally lived the transition from solo practice to group. He put it bluntly:

"I'm a 'call-out-the-elephant-in-the-room type guy.' And the elephant in the room is that doctors are worried Operations are going to lead them. That's been the world for the last quarter century, right?"

And then the reframe that surprises most dentists:

"When we talk about autonomy, actually, it's the opposite of what you'd expect -- the reality is we're going to ask you to lead more. We're going to amplify your voice. Your ideas are going to influence everyone. And theirs are going to influence you."

Clinical autonomy at SGA does not mean you are left alone. It means you are surrounded by peers who challenge you to get better -- on your terms, using your judgment, with your name on every treatment plan.

That is the difference between isolation disguised as independence and genuine clinical freedom surrounded by infrastructure.

Download the SGA Partnership Overview

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Ask any DSO this question: "Show me where clinical autonomy is written in the partnership agreement." If they hesitate, you have your answer.

emails.subject: Email 4
emails.preheader: ** Upfront cash + platform equity + ongoing ownership. Here is the math.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

There is a number your broker will never show you.

It is the difference between what you receive at closing and what your equity could be worth five years later. For some Thurston Group partners, that second number was larger than the first.

Most practice owners fixate on the multiple. That is like evaluating a house by the down payment and ignoring the appreciation over 20 years. Let me show you the full picture.

The most common question practice owners ask -- after autonomy -- is about the money. Not just the valuation. The structure. Most DSOs answer with jargon and slide decks. I will answer in plain language.

The basic structure (simplified):

When you partner with SGA, the deal is structured as a joint-venture equity partnership. Here is what that means:

1. Upfront liquidity. You receive a meaningful cash payment at close -- typically 60-70% of the agreed valuation. This is your liquidity event. It lets you diversify, fund college tuition, pay off debt, or simply sleep better at night knowing your net worth is no longer concentrated in a single illiquid asset.

2. Retained equity in the SGA platform. The remaining 30-40% of the deal value is rolled into ownership shares in the combined SGA entity. This is not a token gesture. It is real equity that appreciates as the platform grows. As one industry analyst put it: "In many cases, the retained minority equity position will be far more valuable in the future than if the doctor had kept 100% of the practice."

3. Ongoing ownership for as long as you are a partner. You hold equity shares in SGA for as long as you remain clinically active. This aligns your incentives with the platform -- when SGA succeeds, you succeed. When your peers grow their practices, your equity benefits.

4. Clinical compensation continues. You continue to earn a competitive salary plus production bonuses. The partnership does not reduce your income -- it supplements it with equity appreciation and removes the costs you were absorbing as an owner (admin staff, HR, compliance, marketing, technology investment).

Why this is structurally different from a traditional DSO sale:

At Heartland, DCA, and many large DSOs, you sell your practice and become an employee. Your upside ends at closing. If the platform grows to 1,000 practices, you do not benefit. You traded ownership for a check and a non-compete.

At SGA, you become a co-owner of the entire platform. When SGA adds its 150th practice, your equity represents a share of 150 practices -- not just yours. The Thurston Group has returned over $4 billion to partners across 8 platforms using this exact model. That is not a projection. It is a repeated pattern.

The question to ask any DSO (including us):

"What happens to my equity when the platform grows? Can I call a current partner and ask them about their experience with the equity model?"

At SGA, the answer to both questions is yes. And we will give you the phone numbers.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- This email is a simplification. The actual deal structure is customized to each practice and involves a detailed evaluation. But the principles are consistent: upfront liquidity, retained equity, and ongoing ownership. No clawbacks. No hidden provisions.

emails.subject: Email 5
emails.preheader: ** 100+ clinicians who actually teach each other. Not consultants. Peers.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Nobody goes into dentistry expecting to be lonely.

But 15 years into practice ownership, loneliness is the problem most dentists feel and fewest name. It does not sound like loneliness -- it sounds like "I have no one to bounce this case off of" or "I wish there was someone who understood what I'm dealing with" or simply the quiet realization that the monthly study club, while nice, does not actually change anything about how you practice.

Dr. Mitch Ellingson, our Chief Clinical Officer, sees this pattern constantly:

"For a lot of docs, they have been on their own for such a long time that they almost forgot how exciting it is to be with peers who are challenging each other to think bigger, to take the next step, to evolve the idea."

This is what SGA's collaborative model is built to solve. Not with team-building exercises or corporate webinars, but with a genuine peer community of 100+ clinicians who actively teach each other.

What this looks like in practice:

- Partner calls where a dentist in Michigan shares a technique with a periodontist in Florida, and both leave with something they did not have before
- The 4Front Symposium, where senior clinicians brainstorm together and "naturally elevated the ideas together. And when you elevate an idea together, that's where the goose bumps come from."
- The Individual Growth Engine, where a mentor does not just give advice -- they open their operatory, share their lab relationships, and walk you through cases together
- A clinical advisory board where your voice shapes the direction of the entire organization

One associate at SGA put it simply: "He relearned how to do implants and now he loves his profession, his patients, everything."

That did not happen because someone handed him a CE stipend. It happened because someone showed up, sat next to him, and taught him.

If you are the kind of dentist who misses having peers who push you -- and you do not even realize how much you miss it until you have it again -- this is worth exploring.

Learn About the 4Front Symposium

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- For associates reading this: the peer community is not reserved for partners. SGA connects new associates with a regional cohort from day one. Residency-like camaraderie without residency-like hours.

emails.subject: Email 6
emails.preheader: ** Capital you cannot access alone. Deployed within 12 months.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

You know the feeling.

A younger dentist down the street just posted about their new CEREC unit on Instagram. Your colleague at the study club is doing guided implant surgery with 3D-printed surgical guides. Meanwhile, you are still taking PVS impressions and hoping the lab gets a clean model.

It is not that you do not know the technology exists. It is that the capital investment -- $100K for a CEREC unit, $150K for a CBCT scanner, $50K for an intraoral scanner, plus the training time to become proficient -- adds up to a number that makes your accountant wince and your spouse nervous.

One practice owner put it this way: "I feel like I'm falling behind. The young dentists coming out of school know digital workflows I've never touched, and I can't afford the time or the money to retool by myself."

Here is what happens when you partner with SGA:

Technology investment is not an afterthought. It is a built-in component of the partnership:

Capital commitment within 12 months. SGA dedicates a capital budget for equipment upgrades at every partner practice. This is not a loan against your future earnings. It is platform capital invested in your practice because a better-equipped practice generates better outcomes for patients, doctors, and the platform.

The right technology, not all technology. SGA does not force a standardized equipment list. The investment is guided by where your practice is and where you want it to go. If implantology is your growth area, the capital goes toward guided surgery systems and CBCT. If restorative is your bread and butter, CEREC and digital impressions are the priority.

Training included. Equipment without training is expensive furniture. SGA provides training through the Pikos Institute (for implant technology), CEREC mentor programs (Dr. Grant Olson is one of only 300 CEREC Mentors worldwide), and peer-to-peer learning from clinicians who have already adopted the workflows.

The number that matters: SGA's partner practices receive $150K-$500K in technology and facility investment within the first 12-18 months, depending on practice needs and growth plans.

That is capital you would have to borrow, save for, or simply forgo if you stayed solo. It is capital SGA deploys because investing in your practice is investing in our platform.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- "Free CEREC training. Can't imagine doing another PVS impression." That is a direct quote from a dentist who joined a DSO with a technology investment program. Imagine never mixing another impression material again.

emails.subject: Email 7
emails.preheader: ** Where the industry is headed -- and where you can position yourself.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Dr. Mitch Ellingson said something that has stuck with me since the first time I read it:

"Dentistry is the last great profession that is run by the people who do it -- the dentists."

That statement is true today. The question is whether it will be true in 2031.

Here is what the landscape looks like:

The consolidation is accelerating. DSO-affiliated practices now represent a significant and growing share of the US dental market. Every major PE firm has a dental platform or is building one. The pace of acquisitions has not slowed despite interest rate increases.

The independent model is under structural pressure. Not because solo dentistry is bad. Because the economics are shifting. Staffing costs are rising faster than reimbursements. Patients expect technology that costs six figures. Compliance requirements grow every year. And the talent -- hygienists, associates, even office managers -- increasingly flows toward organizations that can offer benefits, career paths, and infrastructure that solo practices cannot match.

The DSO model is splitting into two tracks. One track is corporate consolidation -- Heartland, Aspen, and the Gen2 standardizers who will continue to grow by volume and efficiency. The other track is what Dr. Ellingson warned against: "In medicine, as it consolidated it became less about the doctors and patients, and more about the organization."

The other track -- the one SGA represents -- is collaborative consolidation. Clinician-led, education-forward, built around the principle that scale and soul are not mutually exclusive.

What this means for you:

You have three options:

1. Stay solo. Continue to own 100% of a practice that depends entirely on you. Accept the administrative burden, the capital constraints, the recruiting challenges, and the isolation. This is a viable choice -- but it requires clear eyes about the cost.

2. Partner with a corporate DSO. Get the liquidity event. Lose the autonomy. Join the 7 out of 10 who end up frustrated. Hope your specific situation is different.

3. Partner with an organization that was built to be different. One backed by 38 years and $4B+ in returns. One where dentists teach dentists, clinical autonomy is contractual, your name stays on the door, and your equity appreciates as the platform grows. One where you come to get better, not just get bought.

We built SGA for option three. If that resonates:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

If you are not ready yet, I understand. This is a once-in-a-career decision and it deserves time. I will continue to share insights and perspectives as the market evolves.

But know this: the market is not waiting. And the dentists who position themselves intentionally -- rather than reactively -- have the strongest outcomes.

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- "What I would like to do is live up to our name. I would like to gather a group of very smart, very successful dentists together who like to collaborate so we can change the game a little bit." -- Lamonte Jensen, CEO. That is the vision. If it matches yours, the conversation is free.

1. Stay solo. Continue to own 100% of a practice that depends entirely on you. Accept the administrative burden, the capital constraints, the recruiting challenges, and the isolation. This is a viable choice -- but it requires clear eyes about the cost.

2. Partner with a corporate DSO. Get the liquidity event. Lose the autonomy. Join the 7 out of 10 who end up frustrated. Hope your specific situation is different.

3. Partner with an organization that was built to be different. One backed by 38 years and $4B+ in returns. One where dentists teach dentists, clinical autonomy is contractual, your name stays on the door, and your equity appreciates as the platform grows. One where you come to get better, not just get bought.

We built SGA for option three. If that resonates:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

If you are not ready yet, I understand. This is a once-in-a-career decision and it deserves time. I will continue to share insights and perspectives as the market evolves.

But know this: the market is not waiting. And the dentists who position themselves intentionally -- rather than reactively -- have the strongest outcomes.

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- "What I would like to do is live up to our name. I would like to gather a group of very smart, very successful dentists together who like to collaborate so we can change the game a little bit." -- Lamonte Jensen, CEO. That is the vision. If it matches yours, the conversation is free.

sequences.name: Sales Sequence
sequences.description: Triggered when a lead signals high intent (attended webinar, requested valuation, or high lead score). 7 emails over 21 days. Moves from exploration to scheduled discovery call.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** Every DSO sells the deal. SGA sells what happens after.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Every DSO in America tells the same story: the before.

The burnout. The administrative burden. The late nights doing books. The exhaustion of carrying everything alone.

And then they tell you about the deal: the valuation, the multiple, the check.

What nobody tells you is what happens in Year 2.

Because in Year 2, the marketing promises meet reality. The operational support either works or it does not. The "clinical autonomy" either holds or it erodes. The equity either appreciates or it sits in a structure you do not fully understand.

That is why we do not just tell you about the deal. We tell you about the transformation that happens after it.

Dr. Matt Cavendish, Year 2:

Before partnering, Matt was 20 years into private practice in Phoenix. Successful by every external measure. Privately? "Maybe was in a spot of complacency, not being challenged as much, just getting through the day and not enjoying it as much."

Twenty-four months into his partnership with Gen4 (now SGA):

- Revenues trailing twelve months: up 16.5%
- EBITA: beating plan by 2.3 points
- Staff retention: 100%
- Cost of dental supplies: beating plan by 6.4 points
- His word for the experience: "Fun."

"When you've been in dentistry for 20 years like I have, sometimes it's not as fun as it used to be. So to be able to do a complex case like this, with somebody who is also working at a very high skill level -- it is fun."

Dr. Mitchel Godat, Periodontal Associates of Memphis:

"SGA supports us and manages things behind the scenes. Our partnership allows us to focus on changing lives."

His practice name? Still Periodontal Associates of Memphis. His team? Still his team. His patients? They never noticed the change -- except that everything runs better.

The question is not whether the deal looks good on paper. The question is: what does Year 2 look like? Year 5?

At SGA, we can show you. With names and numbers.

If you are ready to explore what Year 2 could look like for your practice:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** No brokers. No NDAs at this stage. Here is exactly what to expect.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

One reason practice owners delay the partnership conversation is that they do not know what it looks like. And the unknown creates anxiety.

Will I be pressured? Will my financial information be shared? Will a broker start calling me three times a day?

Let me remove the ambiguity. Here is exactly how the SGA partnership process works:

Step 1: Connect (Week 1)

You schedule a 30-minute confidential conversation with our partnership team. This is not a sales call. It is a mutual exploration. You tell us about your practice -- your revenue, your team, your clinical focus, your vision. We tell you about SGA -- our structure, our verticals, our education platform.

At the end of the call, you decide whether to continue the conversation. If not, nothing changes. No follow-up pressure. No broker callbacks.

Step 2: Engage (Weeks 2-6)

If there is mutual interest, we walk through a more detailed evaluation. We share how the equity model works for a practice like yours. You share operational and financial data (under NDA at this stage) so we can build an honest valuation.

During this phase, we encourage you to talk directly with current SGA partners. Not our business development team. The actual dentists who made this decision and live with the outcome every day. We will give you their phone numbers.

Step 3: Partner (Weeks 6-12+)

If the fit is right for both sides, we present a formal partnership proposal. You review it with your accountant, your attorney, your financial advisor -- anyone you trust. There is no countdown clock. There is no "this offer expires Friday." A career-defining decision deserves time.

If you move forward, the transition begins: operational handoff, technology investment planning, mentor pairing, and a clear 90-day onboarding timeline for you and your team.

What we do NOT do:

- We do not share your information with brokers
- We do not cold-call you after you say "not right now"
- We do not pressure you with artificial deadlines
- We do not make promises we cannot back with a phone call to a current partner

This is a trust funnel, not a sales funnel. The timeline is yours.

Ready to start with Step 1?

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- The average partnership conversation from first call to signed agreement takes 3-6 months. Some are faster. Some take a year. We move at the pace of your confidence, not ours.

emails.subject: What our partners say when we are not in the room
subjectVariants: "Don't push back. There's no downside to having a mentor."
Five partners. Five practices. One thing in common.
emails.preheader: ** Their names. Their practices. Their words. Not ours.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

I can tell you what SGA offers all day long. But you should not take my word for it.

Here is what our partners say -- in their words, not mine:

---

Dr. Mitchel Godat | Periodontal Associates of Memphis, TN

"We realized that we needed help to get to the next level. We chose SGA because they support us and manage things behind the scenes. Our partnership allows us to focus on changing lives."

What stayed the same: Practice name. Team. Clinical approach. Patient relationships.
What changed: Billing, payroll, HR, and compliance are handled. He focuses on perio.

---

Dr. Matt Cavendish | Phoenix, AZ

"Finding these doctors passionate about dentistry -- well, that might kind of rejuvenate you a bit. Make it fun again."

The numbers: Revenue up 16.5% TTM. EBITA beating plan by 2.3 points. Staff retention at 100%.
The turning point: Observing his first all-on-four with his mentor, Dr. Bryan Shanahan.

---

Dr. Mitch Ellingson | Chief Clinical Officer, Gen4 (now SGA)

"I've personally experienced it. I've been through the transition of going from private-practice dentist to working inside a group, and it is really hard. It's way harder than anybody anticipates."

Why this matters: Our clinical leadership has lived the transition, not just managed it.

---

Dr. Brian Harris | Gen4 Partner

"This organization is fully invested in *us* -- in us loving what we do and being successful in what we do so we can provide excellent care to our patients."

What he noticed: The investment is in the people, not just the spreadsheets.

---

Dr. Grant Olson | Innovative Dental, Springfield, MO (MODIS Partner)

"Implant Dentistry is about giving people back all of life's moments. If you have to think about your teeth, those moments aren't there."

What stayed the same: His 138K-subscriber YouTube channel. His practice name. His team. His spa-like patient experience.
What MODIS added: Capital, recruiting, and operational support to scale what he built.

---

These are not anonymous testimonials. They are named dentists at named practices who have offered to speak directly with prospective partners. If you want a conversation with any of them before you talk to us, just reply to this email.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

emails.subject: Email 4
emails.preheader: ** We have heard every objection. Here are the ones that matter.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

If you are like every practice owner we have spoken to, you are carrying a set of fears about this decision. Not vague anxieties. Specific, concrete fears that surface when you are alone with the idea.

Let me name them. And let me answer them directly.

Fear 1: "I'll lose my autonomy."

You choose your materials, your lab, your schedule, your treatment plans. This is written into the partnership agreement -- not a verbal promise in a meeting, a contractual commitment. Our CCO, Dr. Mitch Ellingson, is a practicing dentist who has personally lived the private-to-group transition. His structural guarantee: "We're going to ask you to lead more. We're going to amplify your voice."

Fear 2: "My patients and staff will freak out."

Dental Partners of SW Georgia is still Dental Partners of SW Georgia. After years. After growth. The name stays. The team stays. The patients never know the difference -- except that everything runs better. SGA operates as an invisible back-office partner, not a consumer-facing brand overlay. Our staff retention rate across the network: 85%.

Fear 3: "The financial terms are a trap."

We have heard the horror stories. "The money, they have a clawback or they had some type of provision that they didn't know about." That is what happens when a first-time fund experiments with dental. Thurston Group has done this 8 times across 38 years. $4B+ returned to partners. We encourage you to bring your attorney, your CPA, and your financial advisor to the table. We welcome their scrutiny because the structure holds up.

Fear 4: "Private equity will flip us in 3 years."

Thurston Group was founded in 1988. They are not a fund on a 5-year clock. They are a firm with a 38-year compounding philosophy. If you want to hear directly from partners at prior Thurston platforms -- Smile Doctors, US Endodontics Partners, US Oral Surgery Management -- we will connect you.

Fear 5: "I'll end up like the 7 out of 10 who regret it."

This is the fear underneath all the others. And it is the reason SGA exists. We studied why those partnerships fail (broken support promises, eroding autonomy, opaque financials) and we built structural safeguards against each one. Not just promises. Contractual commitments, a practicing-dentist CCO, transparent equity models, and named partners you can call.

I cannot eliminate the fear. But I can give you evidence. And people to call.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Reply with the number of the fear that hit closest to home. I will send you the specific evidence that addresses it. Not a brochure. The actual proof point -- a partner's name, a contract clause, or a data point you can verify yourself.

And if you have a fear I did not name, reply with that instead. I will answer it directly.

emails.subject: Email 5
emails.preheader: ** This is not manufactured urgency. It is market reality.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

I do not believe in manufactured urgency. Countdown timers and "last chance" language have no place in a once-in-a-career decision.

But I also cannot ignore what the market is telling us. And right now, the market is telling practice owners something specific:

The window for premium valuations is open. And there are structural reasons it may not stay open indefinitely.

Here are three forces working in your favor right now:

1. PE capital deployment is at peak levels. Dental-focused private equity funds raised record capital in 2023-2024 and are under pressure to deploy it. This creates competition among buyers, which pushes multiples higher. Mid-market practices ($1M-$3M revenue) are commanding 7-9x EBITDA. Groups are reaching 9-12x. These are historically strong numbers.

2. Interest rates are stabilizing. After the rate increases of 2023-2024, the financing environment for dental acquisitions has normalized. SGA recently secured a $350 million credit facility for continued growth. Capital is available and it is being deployed.

3. Your practice is most valuable while you are energized. Practice valuations are driven by EBITDA, growth trajectory, and key-person risk. The longer you carry the administrative burden alone, the more your energy declines, your technology ages, and your team risks attrition. Every year you wait, the practice becomes slightly harder to transition at a premium multiple.

And one force that should give you pause:

Market saturation is approaching. With 1,000+ IDSOs competing for practices, the first wave of acquisitions captured the most attractive targets. The next wave will be more selective and more price-sensitive. Practices that wait until the market shifts from seller-favorable to buyer-favorable will face lower multiples and fewer qualified bidders.

What this means for you specifically:

I cannot tell you what your practice is worth without a conversation. But I can tell you that practice owners who explore partnerships from a position of strength -- while the market favors sellers, while their practice is growing, while they still have energy -- consistently achieve better outcomes than those who wait until they are exhausted and the market has turned.

One more thing I want you to consider.

We evaluate a limited number of partnerships per quarter -- not because of any arbitrary cap, but because every new partner deserves the same onboarding quality that Dr. Cavendish, Dr. Godat, and our other partners received. We will not dilute that for growth.

If your financial advisor is telling you to explore your options in 2026, starting the evaluation conversation now -- even if the decision is 12 months away -- puts you in a stronger position than scrambling when the market shifts.

This is not a "now or never" pitch. It is a market observation from a firm that has done this 8 times over 38 years. Timing matters. And right now, the timing favors the dentist willing to have the conversation.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Our partnership evaluation calendar fills quarterly. If your concern is timing -- "Am I too early? Am I too late?" -- that is exactly what the first conversation addresses. As one dentist told White Coat Investor: "I prefer to be early than late."

emails.subject: Email 6
emails.preheader: ** QuickBooks will never call you to the back office again.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Let me paint you a picture.

It is 6:30 AM. You walk into your practice. Not at 5:45 to review the accounts payable. Not at 6:00 to deal with the email from the insurance company. 6:30. Because the billing team handled the claims. The HR team resolved the staff question. The marketing team sent the patient recall emails. The recruiting team is screening candidates for the hygiene chair that has been empty for four months.

Your morning: review the day's clinical schedule. Sip your coffee. Walk into operatory one and do what you became a dentist to do.

Your lunch: eat lunch. Not while signing payroll. Not while returning insurance calls. Lunch.

Your afternoon: see patients. Discuss a complex case with your mentor from the Individual Growth Engine. Text a colleague you met at the 4Front Symposium about a technique you want to try.

Your evening: home by 5:45. Dinner with your family. Not the laptop open on the dining room table with QuickBooks glowing in the background. Dinner.

One partner described it this way: "I am more engaged with my patients. QuickBooks no longer calls me to the back office."

Another put it even more simply: "I love most of all walking out of the office at the end of the day and immediately transforming into husband and dad."

That is not a fantasy. It is Tuesday at a SGA partner practice.

And here is what did not change: your name is still on the door. Your team is still in the chairs. Your patients still see your face first. The woman at the front desk is the same woman who has been there for twelve years. The community still knows it as your practice.

The only difference: you are not carrying it alone anymore.

If you are ready to stop carrying it alone:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- "I traded feeling tired and being up late doing books for late-night diaper changes and feedings." That is a real quote from a dentist who partnered. He chose what keeps him up at night. So can you.

emails.subject: Email 7
emails.preheader: ** This is not a sequence. It is an invitation that stays open.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Over the past several weeks, I have shared industry data, partner stories, proof points, and honest answers to the fears every practice owner carries.

I have told you about the Thurston Group's 38 years and $4B+ returned to partners. About Dr. Cavendish's rejuvenation after 20 years of practice. About Dr. Godat keeping his name, his team, and his focus on changing lives. About an education platform anchored by Dr. Michael Pikos and 100+ clinicians who actively teach each other.

I have not been subtle about what I believe: SGA is the partnership platform that was built for dentists who refuse to choose between clinical excellence and financial security. Between keeping what they built and getting the support they need. Between their identity and their sanity.

But I have not asked you the only question that matters:

What would it mean for you -- personally, professionally, financially -- to stop carrying it alone?

I am not asking you to sell your practice. I am asking you to have a conversation with someone who has sat where you sit and can show you what the other side looks like.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** Class IIs all day? There is another way.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Your Career Decision Guide is ready. Download it here:

Download My Career Guide

Take 15 minutes with it. The guide covers the real comparison between solo practice, corporate DSO associateships, and partnership-track positions -- with actual numbers, not "competitive compensation" filler language. It also includes the questions you should ask in any dental job interview, including the ones most organizations hope you will not think to ask.

Now, a question for you.

When you walked into dental school on day one, what kind of dentist did you picture becoming?

I am guessing it was not this: 25-30 patients a day. Fifteen minutes per patient. A schedule template that someone else designed. No time for the implant case you know you could handle if anyone gave you the chance. A production bonus that incentivizes speed, not skill.

One associate at a corporate DSO described it this way: "I feel like a tooth mechanic, not a doctor. I didn't go through four years of dental school plus residency to do nothing but Class IIs all day."

If that sounds familiar, you are not alone. And you are not stuck.

SGA Dental Partners was built on a simple premise: your career is not a production number. It is a trajectory. And the trajectory should be designed around where you want to go clinically -- not just how many patients you can see before 5:30.

Over the next few emails, I am going to show you what that looks like in practice. Not with promises. With names, programs, and outcomes.

Talk soon,

Sender name
SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- If you already know what you are looking for and want to skip ahead to open positions: View Current Opportunities. No pressure either way.

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** This is not a CE stipend. It is someone who shows up and teaches you.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Every DSO job posting says "mentorship." Most of them mean: a one-week orientation, a login to an online CE portal, and a senior dentist who is too busy with their own patients to answer your questions.

Here is what mentorship actually looks like at SGA.

The Individual Growth Engine.

When you join SGA as an associate, you are paired with an experienced partner clinician. Not a corporate trainer reading a script. A practicing dentist who has been where you are and can show you what they see.

This is not optional. It is structural. The mentor does not just answer questions when you happen to have one. They open their operatory, share their lab relationships, walk you through cases together, and help you build a custom clinical development plan based on where YOU want to go -- not where the schedule needs you.

One associate's story:

He joined Gen4 (now part of SGA) as a general dentist doing the same bread-and-butter cases he had been doing for years. Through the Individual Growth Engine, he was paired with a mentor who specialized in implants and complex restorative.

The result: "He relearned how to do implants and now he loves his profession, his patients, everything."

That is not a salary increase. That is a career transformation. The kind of change that makes you excited to walk into the operatory instead of dreading the schedule.

Dr. Matt Cavendish's parallel:

Cavendish was not a new associate -- he was a 20-year veteran. But the mentorship model worked the same way. His mentor, Dr. Bryan Shanahan, invited him to observe his first all-on-four. He shared surgical techniques, treatment planning approaches, and lab relationships that Cavendish never would have accessed alone.

"Just to pick his brain on his surgical technique, treatment planning, and the way he works with his lab -- that's something I would have never had an opportunity to do if I were still in a solo practice."

His advice: "Don't push back. There's no downside to having a mentor."

The Pikos Institute connection.

If implantology is where your growth interest lies, SGA gives you access to the Pikos Institute -- Dr. Michael A. Pikos's world-renowned education center for hands-on implant training. This is not a webinar. This is five days in a surgical suite with one of the most respected implantologists in global dentistry.

A standalone Pikos course costs $6,500-$12,000. For SGA associates, it is part of the platform.

The CE stipend comparison:

Corporate DSO: $1,500-$3,000/year for generic CE
SGA: Pikos Institute + 4Front Symposium + Individual Growth Engine + peer network of 100+ clinicians

Total quantified value of SGA's clinical development program: $37,000-$70,000/year above what a corporate DSO provides. That is not a benefits line. It is a career accelerator.

Explore Associate Opportunities

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- For the record: you will still do bread-and-butter cases at SGA. Every practice needs them. The difference is that bread-and-butter is the floor, not the ceiling.

emails.subject: Email 3
emails.preheader: ** Your skills are not the problem. Your environment is.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Let me tell you about a dentist named Grant Olson.

He grew up in Springfield, Missouri -- population 170,000, four hours from the nearest major city. He was going to be an accountant. Then he discovered dentistry.

He did not leave Springfield. He could have. His clinical credentials -- one of only 300 CEREC Mentors worldwide, patented guided implant technology, case complexity that rivals university programs -- would have opened doors in LA, New York, or any dental school in the country.

Instead, he built Innovative Dental in his hometown. A practice that looks like a spa, not a dental office. A concierge who walks patients through a "care itinerary." Technology that most dental schools do not have.

Then he turned on a camera.

138,000 YouTube subscribers. 23 million views. 3,390+ five-star reviews. Patients who fly in from across the country. No media company. No PR agency. Just a dentist talking directly to people about their teeth.

"What's up, guys? This is Dr. O from Innovative Dental."

Dr. Olson is a MODIS partner within the SGA platform. His practice retained its name, its team, its patient experience, its YouTube channel. What MODIS added: capital, recruiting support, operational infrastructure, and a network of specialist peers.

Why this matters to you:

Dr. Olson is where you could be in 10 years if you choose the right environment today. Not because SGA guarantees that outcome -- nobody can. But because SGA provides the environment that makes it possible:

- Complex cases from day one. SGA partner practices range from community general dentistry to advanced implantology. As an associate, you are placed where your clinical interests align -- and you grow into cases you could not access at a high-volume corporate practice.

- Technology access. SGA invests $150K-$500K in technology at partner practices. That means CEREC, guided implant systems, CBCT scanners, and digital workflows -- equipment you use, not just read about.

- Cross-practice learning. When a dentist in Michigan masters a technique, they share it with the network. When a periodontist in Florida develops a protocol, SGA associates can learn from it. The 4Front Symposium and partner calls make this real, not theoretical.

- Patient volume that matches your growth. SGA's centralized marketing drives new patients to partner practices. You will not sit in an empty operatory. But you also will not be on a 25-patient-per-day treadmill with 15-minute appointments.

Your clinical skills are not the problem. Your environment is.

Explore Associate Opportunities

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Dr. Olson signs off every video the same way: "Keep smiling... it looks good on you." That is not a corporate tagline. It is the voice of a dentist who built something extraordinary in a small town. It is possible.

emails.subject: Email 4
emails.preheader: ** Not a vague promise. A structure with numbers.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Every dental job posting says "pathway to partnership." Almost none of them explain what that means.

Here is what it means at SGA:

You can invest in the company as a Class A shareholder from your first day.

Read that again. Not after 3 years. Not after hitting a production target. Not "when a spot opens up." From day one. You have the option to purchase Class A shares in SGA, making you a co-owner of the entire platform alongside every partner dentist in the network.

This is not a token gesture. It is real equity that appreciates as the platform grows. When SGA adds its 150th practice, your shares represent a stake in 150 practices -- not just the one you work in.

What the career pathway looks like:

| Stage | Timeline | What Happens | What You Build |
|-------|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Onboarding | Months 1-6 | Mentor assigned, clinical systems training, peer cohort connection, 30/60/90-day check-ins | Foundation and relationships |
| Clinical development | Months 6-24 | Individual Growth Engine: custom training plan, Pikos Institute access, 4Front Symposium, complex case mentorship | Clinical skills that compound |
| Partnership track | Year 2+ | Increasing clinical autonomy, Class A share accumulation, leadership development, expanded case responsibility | Wealth and professional identity |
| Full partnership | Year 5+ | Equity stake in SGA platform, potential practice leadership, clinical advisory board eligibility | The career you envisioned |

The math your corporate DSO will not show you:

At a corporate DSO, you earn a salary and a production bonus. When you leave (and most associates do within 2-3 years), you take nothing with you except a W-2.

At SGA, you earn a competitive salary ($160K-$240K depending on experience), a production bonus (28-32% above threshold), full benefits (401(k) with match, Cigna, PTO, CE) -- AND equity that appreciates over time.

The associate who invests in Class A shares in Year 1 and stays through Year 7 has not just earned a salary. They have built an ownership position in a multi-state dental platform backed by the Thurston Group's 38-year track record.

$340K in student debt feels different when your equity is growing alongside your loan payoff.

The question to ask any employer:

"Can I invest in the company from day one? And can you show me what that investment has been worth for associates who made it three years ago?"

At SGA, the answer is yes to both.

Explore Associate Opportunities

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- Your financial advisor will want to understand the Class A share structure in detail. We welcome that conversation. Bring them. We have nothing to hide.

emails.subject: Email 5
emails.preheader: ** Residency-like camaraderie without residency-like hours.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Can I be direct?

The loneliest job in dentistry is not the solo practice owner. It is the associate at a high-volume practice who sees 25 patients a day and has no one to talk to about any of them.

No mentor. No peer group. No one to text at lunch when a case does not look right on the X-ray. The owner comes in two days a week. The other associates are too busy with their own schedules. You are, functionally, alone.

"I feel totally isolated. There's no one to bounce cases off of or ask for help."

That is not a weakness. It is a structural failure of most dental work environments. And it is fixable.

What SGA's peer community looks like for associates:

Regional cohort connection. When you join SGA, you are introduced to a cohort of associates at similar career stages across the network. Think of it as your professional peer group -- people who understand your challenges because they are living them too. Residency-like camaraderie without residency-like hours.

The 4Front Symposium. SGA's annual gathering of partner clinicians. Not a corporate conference with slide decks and boxed lunches. A room full of dentists who actively teach each other, share what they have learned, and challenge each other to think bigger. "Senior clinicians brainstorming together naturally elevated the ideas together. That's where the goose bumps come from."

Your mentor. Through the Individual Growth Engine, you have someone who is invested in your growth -- not as a line item on an HR report, but as a clinical colleague who remembers what it felt like to be in your shoes.

Partner calls and case collaboration. Regular touchpoints across the network where real cases are discussed, techniques are shared, and the isolation of solo practice dissolves.

One associate described the change simply: "He relearned how to do implants and now he loves his profession, his patients, everything."

That did not come from a CE stipend. It came from belonging.

Here is what I want to leave you with:

Over these five emails I have shared what SGA offers that your current position almost certainly does not:

- Clinical mentorship through the Individual Growth Engine and Pikos Institute
- Complex cases and technology access that expand your skills, not shrink them
- A real equity pathway with Class A shares from day one
- A peer community of 100+ clinicians who challenge each other to grow

Your career is not a production number. It is a trajectory. And it should be aimed at the dentist you envisioned becoming on the first day of dental school.

If that dentist is someone who does complex cases, who has a mentor, who owns equity in something larger than a single practice, and who walks into the operatory energized instead of drained -- let me introduce you to the people who can make that happen.

Apply to SGA

Or, if you want to talk first:

Schedule a Call with Our Recruiting Team

Talk soon,

Sender name
SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- Dr. Mitch Ellingson, our CCO, said something that captures what we are building: "We want to take each individual where they want to go so that it's not just a job; it is a career." If that sounds like what you have been looking for, you have found it.

sequences.name: Re-engagement Sequence
sequences.description: Triggered after 60+ days of email inactivity. 5 emails over 21 days. Re-establishes relevance and offers fresh value to dormant leads.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** We are still here. And things have moved.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

It has been a while since we heard from you. That is completely fine.

A decision about your practice's future is not something you rush. The average partnership conversation takes 6-18 months from first interest to signed agreement, and some dentists take longer. The timeline is yours.

But I wanted to check in for a simple reason: the landscape has shifted since we last connected, and some of what has changed might matter to you.

Rather than assume what is on your mind, I have a quick question:

What is the biggest priority for you right now?

  I am still evaluating partnership options -- send me what is new
  My situation has changed -- I am not exploring partnerships right now
  I am ready to have a conversation
  I would rather receive less email from SGA

Click the one that fits and I will adjust accordingly. No sales pitch. No follow-up call. Just the right information at the right cadence.

Click to Answer

Talk soon,

Sender name

P.S. -- If you clicked "ready to have a conversation," here is the direct link to schedule: Schedule a Confidential Conversation. If you clicked anything else, I will respect that completely.

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** We have not been standing still. Here is the evidence.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

While you have been focused on your practice (as you should be), a few things have happened at SGA that are worth knowing about:

1. The platform grew.

For you, this means: the platform your equity would be part of is larger and more diversified than it was when we last connected.

sequences.name: Soap Opera Sequence
sequences.description: Story-driven emotional engagement sequence for practice owners. 5 emails over 10 days. Uses narrative tension and character development to create deep emotional connection with SGA's mission.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** What I discovered after going through it myself surprised me.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

I need to tell you something that most people in my position would never admit.

My name is Dr. Mitch Ellingson. I am a dentist. Not "was a dentist" -- am. I still practice. I still see patients. I still pick up a handpiece every week. That matters, and I will explain why in a moment.

But I am also the Chief Clinical Officer of SGA Dental Partners. And before that, I co-founded Gen4 Dental Partners. And before *that*, I owned multiple practices in Phoenix and founded Spear Practice Solutions.

So I have been on both sides of this conversation.

I have been the practice owner lying awake on Sunday night, dreading Monday because the office manager just quit and the schedule is a disaster and nobody else is going to fix it. I have been the guy doing payroll at 10 PM when he should have been watching his kids' soccer highlights. I have been the dentist who loved the craft and slowly started to resent the business.

And I have been through the transition. The one where you go from being king of your own island to being part of something larger. The one where every colleague tells you you are crazy, and every forum post warns you about selling your soul.

I did it anyway.

What I discovered surprised me.

Not the operational stuff -- I expected the relief of someone else handling HR and billing and compliance. That part was exactly what they promised.

What surprised me was something nobody warned me about. Something I had lost so gradually I did not even realize it was gone until it came back.

I will tell you what it was in my next email. But first, I want you to sit with a question:

When was the last time you were genuinely excited about a Monday morning in your practice?

Not "okay with it." Not "pushing through." Genuinely excited.

If you cannot remember, you are not failing. You are carrying something that was never supposed to be carried alone.

More tomorrow.

Mitch Ellingson, DDS
Chief Clinical Officer
SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- If you already know you want to have a direct conversation instead of waiting for the rest of this story, I respect that. You can schedule a confidential call here: Schedule a Confidential Conversation. No brokers. No pressure. Just two dentists talking.

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** Seven out of ten. That number kept me up at night.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Yesterday I told you something surprised me about my own transition from solo practice to group partnership.

Before I reveal what it was, I need to tell you about the thing that almost stopped me from making the move at all.

It was a conversation at a dental conference. Could have been Hinman. Could have been a state meeting. The venue does not matter. What matters is what a colleague told me over dinner.

He had sold his practice to a DSO eighteen months earlier. On paper, the deal was excellent. Strong multiple. Clean terms. Big check.

And he was miserable.

"Mitch," he said, "I wish I would have never done this."

I asked him what went wrong. He told me the support they promised never materialized. The "clinical autonomy" language in the contract meant nothing once the regional manager started sending emails about production targets. His long-tenured hygienist left within six months because the benefits got worse. His office manager -- the woman who had been with him for fifteen years -- was "restructured" out because corporate had their own systems.

His name was still on the door. But his practice did not feel like his practice anymore.

I wish I could tell you his was an isolated story. It was not.

The Dentist Advisors podcast put a number on it that still haunts me: seven or eight out of ten dentists who sell to a DSO end up frustrated, annoyed, or outright regretful. "It's not what they thought."

Seven out of ten.

I started paying attention after that dinner. I started asking every dentist I met who had joined a DSO: how is it going? Really?

The pattern was always the same. The check cleared. The relief lasted about ninety days. Then the calls started -- from people who had never held a handpiece telling you which lab to use, which materials to order, how many patients to see per hour. One dentist told me: "Decisions are being made about what your production is supposed to be by people wearing suits in a boardroom."

The staff left. The culture died. "Dr. Jones is gone, there's been 2 to 3 new doctors there, all the old staff are gone." That is a *patient* talking. Not a dentist. A patient who noticed that the practice they trusted had been quietly hollowed out.

I watched this happen to good dentists. Smart dentists. Colleagues I respected. Not because they made bad decisions -- because the model they sold into was designed to extract, not to partner.

And I thought: it does not have to be this way.

That thought became a conviction. The conviction became a company. But the moment it all crystallized -- the specific epiphany -- happened in a room I did not expect.

I will tell you about that room on Monday.

For now, I want to leave you with this: if you have been googling "sell dental practice" and every result fills you with a mix of hope and dread, you are not paranoid. You are paying attention. The dread is earned. The industry has earned it.

But the hope is legitimate too. I have seen the other side.

More soon.

Mitch

P.S. -- There is a line I keep coming back to. I told it to Lamonte Jensen the first time we sat down together: "I love being a dentist, and I want to protect my profession from becoming like medicine. In medicine, as it consolidated it became less about the doctors and patients, and more about the organization."

That is why I do this. That is why I am writing to you. Not to sell you something. To protect something.
