emails.subject: Email 3
emails.preheader: ** A Marine, a dentist, and a 38-year track record walked into the same room.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

I promised I would tell you about the room.

It was not a boardroom. It was not a conference hall. It was a working session -- papers everywhere, whiteboards covered in diagrams, coffee that had been sitting too long. The kind of room where real things get built.

At the table: Lamonte Jensen. Me. And the team from the Thurston Group.

Now, you probably have not heard of the Thurston Group. Most dentists have not. They do not run television ads or sponsor dental conferences for visibility. What they do is build healthcare platforms.

They have done it for 38 years. They have returned more than four billion dollars to their partners across eight platforms. Smile Doctors. US Endodontics Partners. US Oral Surgery Management. And South Georgia Dental Management -- which is where the original SGA started.

That is not a pitch. Those are names you can look up. Numbers you can verify. A track record that predates most DSOs by decades.

But here is what mattered to me in that room. It was not the track record. It was the *question* they asked.

Every DSO conversation I had ever been part of started the same way: "What is the practice worth? What are the multiples? How fast can we close?"

The Thurston team started with a different question: "What went wrong with every other model, and why?"

We spent two days tearing apart the Gen1 accounting machines. The Gen2 cookie-cutter standardizers -- "same supplies in the same place in every office." The Gen3 buying groups that had good intentions and no scale. We looked at why seven out of ten dentists end up regretting the decision. Not the symptoms. The structural causes.

And somewhere in the middle of day two, a thought formed that I have not been able to shake since.

What if we did not just acquire practices -- what if we made the dentists inside them *better*?

Not "better supported." Not "better managed." Actually better at the craft of dentistry.

What if the competitive moat was not back-office efficiency or purchasing power or standardized systems -- but *education*? What if we built a platform where a general dentist in Phoenix could observe his first all-on-four procedure alongside a mentor who makes complex cases fun? Where a periodontist could train at the Pikos Institute -- alongside Dr. Michael Pikos himself -- and bring those skills back to patients who would have been referred out or underserved?

What if the thing that made a dentist choose us was not the check we wrote them at closing, but the dentist they became in year two, year three, year five?

That was the epiphany. And we were so convinced it represented a generational shift in dental consolidation that we put it in the company name.

Gen4. The fourth generation.

Lamonte -- a Marine Corps infantry officer with combat tours in Iraq, an MBA from the University of Chicago, a McKinsey consultant, and a former VP at Spear Education who had seen firsthand what dentists actually struggle with -- looked at the whiteboard and said something I have never forgotten:

"I would like to gather a group of very smart, very successful dentists together who like to collaborate so we can change the game a little bit."

We went from 5 group practices to 90 in 20 months. Not because we had the best pitch. Because dentists were starving for a model that treated them as partners, not products.

But what happened next -- the part I promised to tell you two emails ago, the thing that surprised me about my own transition -- is something I did not expect at all.

It was not about the business. It was deeply personal. And it happened to Dr. Matt Cavendish in a way that I think will resonate with you.

That story is next.

Mitch

P.S. -- I told you in my first email that I still practice. I still pick up a handpiece every week. I said that matters, and I promised to explain why. Here is why: I am not writing to you from a corner office. I am writing from the same world you inhabit. I know what it feels like to have a case go sideways at 3 PM and still have two hours of patients on the schedule. I know what it sounds like when the compressor makes a noise it should not be making. I know the specific kind of tired you feel when you love the work but hate everything surrounding it. That is why I co-founded this. Not to manage dentists. To protect the profession I love.

That is why I co-founded this. Not to manage dentists. To protect the profession I love.

emails.subject: Email 4
emails.preheader: ** Revenue up 16.5%. Staff retention at 100%. But that is not the number he talks about most.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

Three emails ago, I told you something surprised me about the transition from solo practice to partnership. Something nobody warned me about. Something I had lost so gradually I did not realize it was gone.

Here is the answer: it was the spark.

I do not mean motivation. I do not mean "engagement" or whatever corporate HR calls it. I mean the specific feeling of walking into a case and thinking *this is going to be interesting* instead of *let me get through this day.*

I had been practicing for thirteen years when I made the transition. Somewhere around year ten, the cases stopped being interesting. Not because the cases changed -- because I had stopped growing. There was no one challenging me. No one showing me a technique I had not tried. No one asking, "Have you considered doing it this way?"

I was clinically competent and professionally bored. And I did not have a word for it until I saw it happen to someone else.

His name is Dr. Matt Cavendish. Phoenix. Twenty years in private practice.

When Matt joined Gen4, he described himself with a honesty that most dentists would not risk in public: "Maybe I was in a spot of complacency, not being challenged as much, just getting through the day and not enjoying it as much."

Twenty years. Getting through the day. Sound familiar?

Here is what we did. We paired Matt with a mentor -- Dr. Bryan Shanahan -- through a program we call the Individual Growth Engine. Not a corporate training module. Not a webinar series. A one-on-one clinical mentorship with another practice owner who is operating at a level Matt had not yet reached.

Matt observed his first all-on-four procedure alongside Bryan. Watched the surgical technique. Learned how Bryan worked with his lab. Picked apart the treatment planning approach. Things Matt would never have been exposed to in a solo practice, because in a solo practice, there is nobody to observe. There is nobody to ask.

"That's something I would have never had an opportunity to do if I were still in a solo practice," Matt said.

Then the numbers followed.

Revenue TTM: up 16.5%.
EBITA: beating plan by 2.3 points. Beating prior year by $45,266.
Total payroll expenses: down.
Staff retention: 100%.
Cost of dental supplies: beating plan by 6.4 points.

Those numbers are real. They are Matt's. And they are impressive.

But that is not the number Matt talks about most.

The number Matt talks about is not on any spreadsheet. It is the number of times he has said "it is fun" in the last twelve months. "When you've been in dentistry for 20 years like I have, sometimes it's not as fun as it used to be. So to be able to do a complex case like this, with somebody who is also working at a very high skill level -- it is fun."

The spark came back.

And here is the hidden benefit -- the one I did not see coming, the one that the DSO brochures never mention because it cannot be reduced to a bullet point:

When you stop being the smartest person in your practice -- when you are surrounded by peers who challenge you to think bigger, take the next step, evolve the idea -- something physiological happens. Ellingson's word for it is "goose bumps." Senior clinicians brainstorming together at the 4Front Symposium, naturally elevating ideas collectively. "That's where the goose bumps come from. That's where you go, 'Wow, okay!' They're like, 'I've never thought about it like that.'"

That is not an operational benefit. That is not a financial benefit. That is a *human* benefit. It is the antidote to the isolation that every solo practitioner carries but rarely names.

And it only works because the dentists in the room are owners, not employees. Partners, not subordinates. People who chose to be there because they were hungry for exactly this.

I have a few more things to tell you. About what the equity model actually means -- not just the financial structure, but the second bite of the apple that most dentists miss. About the career pathways that exist inside SGA now that the three verticals are combined. About the 85% staff retention rate that nobody in this industry publishes because nobody else wants to be measured by it.

But those are details. The real question is the one I started with:

Are you still excited about Monday morning?

If the answer is no, it may not be because you chose the wrong profession. It may be because you have been carrying it alone for too long.

Tomorrow I will tell you why the timing of this matters more than you think.

Mitch

P.S. -- Matt Cavendish offered one piece of advice for any dentist considering a partnership: "Don't push back. There's no downside to having a mentor." From a twenty-year practice owner, that is not a small statement. That is a man who has swallowed his pride and come out better for it.

emails.subject: Email 5
emails.preheader: ** This is not manufactured scarcity. This is market data.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

I have spent the last week and a half telling you a story. My story. Matt Cavendish's story. The story of what happens when dentists stop carrying the weight alone.

Today, I need to talk about timing. Not because I am trying to manufacture urgency -- I despise that tactic and you would see through it anyway. But because the market is moving in ways that make this conversation more consequential with every quarter that passes.

Here is what I mean.

The consolidation wave is not slowing down. It is accelerating.

There are over 1,000 invisible dental support organizations operating in the US right now. But fewer than 100 are considered qualified bidders by industry experts. That means over 900 organizations are making offers to practice owners with neither the capital, the infrastructure, nor the track record to deliver on their promises.

Every quarter, hundreds of practices change hands. Most of those transactions look fine on day one. Eighteen months later, the Dentist Advisors statistic kicks in: seven out of ten end up frustrated or regretful.

The talent crisis is reshaping the leverage.

Over 76% of practices find hygienist recruiting "extremely challenging." The best hygienists, the best associates, the best office managers are flowing toward organizations that offer 401(k) plans, real healthcare benefits, PTO, CE stipends, and career development paths. Solo practices are losing the recruiting war not because they are bad places to work, but because they cannot compete on infrastructure.

Every quarter you wait, the recruiting gap widens. And a practice that cannot fill its hygiene chairs sees revenue drop 15-20%. That revenue decline depresses your valuation at the exact moment you may need it most.

The practices getting the strongest partnership terms are the ones who come to the table from a position of strength -- not desperation.

This is the part most dentists get backwards. They wait until they are burned out, until the office manager has left, until the compressor has failed and the roof needs replacing and the practice value has softened. Then they start having conversations from a position of weakness. And they get weak terms.

The dentists who get the best partnerships are the ones who start the conversation while their practice is thriving. While their team is intact. While their revenue is climbing. They come to the table with leverage, and they use it.

I am not asking you to make a decision today.

A partnership of this magnitude -- one that involves your life's work, your team, your patients, your financial future -- is not a decision that should be rushed. The timeline from first conversation to signed agreement is typically 6-18 months, and it should be. This is not a transaction. It is a relationship.

What I am asking is simpler: have the conversation now, while you have options.

Here is what that looks like:

You schedule a confidential call with our partnership team. Thirty minutes. No brokers, no NDAs at this stage, no commitment of any kind. You ask every hard question you have been carrying -- about autonomy, about equity, about what changes and what does not. And we answer them. With names. With numbers. With the phone numbers of current partners who will tell you exactly how it has been for them.

If it is not the right fit, you will know within thirty minutes. And you will leave the conversation more informed about your options than you were before it.

If it is the right fit, you will have started a process while your practice is strong, your team is intact, and your leverage is at its highest.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

I have one more thing to say, and then I will stop filling your inbox with long emails.

I told you in my first message that I still practice. That I still see patients. That I still pick up a handpiece.

I do this because dentistry is the last great profession that is still run by the people who do it. Not administrators. Not algorithms. Not suits in a boardroom. Dentists. And I want to keep it that way.

If that resonates with you -- if the reason you have read five emails from a stranger is because somewhere in this story you saw a version of yourself -- then the conversation is worth having.

Not because I am selling you something. Because the profession we both love is worth protecting. And protecting it starts with choosing the right partner.

Mitch Ellingson, DDS
Chief Clinical Officer
SGA Dental Partners

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

P.S. -- I promised you names and numbers. Here are a few to start:

- Dr. Mitchel S. Godat, Periodontal Associates of Memphis: "SGA supports us and manages things behind the scenes. Our partnership allows us to focus on changing lives."
- Dr. Matt Cavendish, Phoenix: Revenue up 16.5%. Staff retention 100%. "It is fun."
- The Thurston Group: 38 years. 8 dental and healthcare platforms. $4 billion returned to partners. Not projections. History.
- SGA Network: 85% staff retention rate. Published, because we are not afraid to be measured.

These are not anonymous testimonials. These are people you can talk to. Ask us to connect you. We will.

sequences.name: Invisible Selling Machine Sequence
sequences.description: Values-driven relationship sequence for practice owners. 7 emails over 28 days. Builds trust through shared values, social proof, and identity-based selling without direct pitch.

emails.subject: Email 1
emails.preheader: ** Dentistry is the last great profession. Here is how we plan to keep it that way.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

You are going to receive a handful of emails from us over the next few weeks. Before any of that begins, you deserve to know why -- and what we believe.

We are SGA Dental Partners -- a dental partnership platform backed by the Thurston Group. We exist to do one thing: make dentists better. Not "better managed." Actually better at the craft and the business of dentistry, through peer collaboration, world-class education, and operational support that frees you to focus on the work that matters.

A mission statement is just words unless it is rooted in conviction. So here is what we actually believe:

We believe dentistry is the last great profession still run by the people who do it.

Medicine lost this. Pharmacy lost it. Optometry is losing it. The pattern was always the same: consolidation promised efficiency and delivered homogenization. The doctors lost control. The patient became a number. The craft became a factory.

We are watching the early stages of this happen in dentistry right now -- one corporate acquisition at a time, one production quota at a time, one practice losing its name and becoming Unit 47 in a national chain.

We refuse to participate in that.

We believe consolidation does not have to mean homogenization.

You can build scale by making dentists better instead of making them interchangeable. Preserve the name on the door, the team in the chairs, the way a doctor chooses to practice -- and surround all of that with operational infrastructure, education, mentorship, and capital that no solo practice can replicate. That is what we built.

We believe the proof is in the names, not the claims.

We will not ask you to trust a tagline. We will give you names: Dr. Mitchel Godat, Periodontal Associates of Memphis. Dr. Matt Cavendish, Phoenix. Dr. Grant Olson, Innovative Dental, Springfield, Missouri. We will give you numbers: 38 years of Thurston Group history. $4B+ returned to partners. 85% staff retention rate. Every claim we make comes with a name attached to it.

Over the next few weeks, we are going to share:

- What your practice broker probably will not tell you about DSO valuations
- Our actual staff retention rate (and why most DSOs refuse to publish theirs)
- What the first 90 days of a partnership actually look like
- A transformation story that may sound uncomfortably familiar

None of it will be a sales pitch. All of it will be useful whether you ever talk to us or not.

If at any point you want to skip ahead and have a direct conversation:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

If you want to keep reading and decide in your own time, we respect that completely. We will be here.

SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- One more belief, the one that drives everything else: "I love being a dentist, and I want to protect my profession from becoming like medicine." That is from Dr. Mitch Ellingson, our Chief Clinical Officer. He is a practicing dentist. He still sees patients every week. He built this because he means it.

emails.subject: Email 2
emails.preheader: ** We have walked away from deals because of this answer.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

This is Mitch Ellingson. I am a practicing dentist and the Chief Clinical Officer at SGA.

I want to tell you about a question we ask every dentist who explores a partnership with us. It is not about revenue. It is not about EBITDA or patient volume or payer mix. Those numbers matter, but they come later.

The first question is this:

"What do you want your practice to look like in five years -- and what is stopping you from getting there alone?"

That is it.

No trick. No formula. Just a question that tells us everything about whether this person is right for SGA.

Here is why.

We have turned down partnerships with profitable practices. Practices doing $3M, $4M, even more. Not because the numbers were wrong -- because the fit was wrong.

If a dentist's answer is "I just want to cash out and coast until retirement," we are the wrong partner. We do not buy practices so dentists can check out. We partner with dentists who want to keep building -- who see the partnership as the beginning of a new chapter, not the final page.

If a dentist's answer is "I want someone to handle the business so I can get back to clinical work, and I want to be surrounded by people who make me better" -- that is our dentist.

Dr. Brian Harris said it better than I can: "This organization is fully invested in *us* -- in us loving what we do and being successful in what we do so we can provide excellent care to our patients."

That is not a corporate talking point from a press release. Brian said that because he feels it every day. Because the partnership was not a transaction -- it was an alignment.

I will be direct with you about something else.

I am what I call a "call-out-the-elephant-in-the-room type guy." And the elephant in every DSO conversation is this: doctors are worried Operations are going to lead them, and operations teams are worried doctors are going to push back. That has been the dynamic for the last quarter century.

We structured SGA to break that cycle. Our clinical advisory board is made up of practicing partner dentists. The Individual Growth Engine pairs doctors with clinical mentors, not corporate trainers. The 4Front Symposium is a room full of peers, not a corporate conference with scripted presentations.

When we talk about autonomy, actually, it is the opposite of what most dentists fear. We are not going to tell you what you can and cannot do. We are going to ask you to *lead more*. We are going to amplify your voice. Your ideas are going to influence everyone. And theirs are going to influence you.

That is the exact opposite of going it alone.

So here is my question to you, Dr. {{first_name}}:

What do you want your practice to look like in five years?

You do not need to answer me. Answer yourself. And if the answer involves growth, collaboration, and getting back to the part of dentistry that made you choose this profession -- we should probably talk.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Mitch

P.S. -- I mentioned we have walked away from deals. One more thing we have never done: we have never asked a partner to change their practice name, replace their team, or follow a corporate clinical protocol. Dental Partners of SW Georgia is still Dental Partners of SW Georgia. Periodontal Associates of Memphis is still Periodontal Associates of Memphis. After five years. After ten years. That is not a policy. It is a principle.

emails.subject: 3 things your practice broker will not tell you about DSO valuations
subjectVariants: The valuation conversation your broker is having without you
What "7-9x EBITDA" actually means for your bank account
emails.preheader: ** This email is useful even if you never talk to us.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

This email is designed to be useful whether you ever partner with SGA or not. Print it. Save it. Forward it to a colleague who is getting approached by brokers. The information is the same regardless.

Three things your practice broker probably will not tell you about DSO valuations:

---

1. "7-9x EBITDA" does not mean what you think it means.

You have heard the multiples. Mid-market practices (1-3 doctors, $1M-$3M revenue) are trading at 7-9x EBITDA. Group practices with 4+ doctors are commanding 9-12x. Those numbers sound impressive.

But here is what the headline multiple obscures:

The multiple is applied to *adjusted* EBITDA, not to the number on your P&L. Every DSO adjusts differently. Some add back your above-market salary. Some subtract projected capital expenditures. Some apply a management fee that reduces the EBITDA before the multiple is applied -- so a practice that earns $500K in EBITDA might get valued on $350K after "adjustments."

One dentist described it on the Dentist Advisors podcast: "Some dentists believe they sold their practice for 4-6x EBITDA, but the DSO manipulated the math based on the note the dentist carries and the management fee."

The number that matters is not the multiple. It is the net proceeds after all adjustments, holdbacks, and earn-out conditions. Ask any DSO you are talking to for the walk-through calculation. If they cannot provide it in plain language, that tells you something.

---

2. The equity you retain may be worth more than the check you deposit.

Most practice owners focus on the upfront check. That is natural. It is the number you can see and touch.

But in a joint-venture equity partnership -- the model SGA uses -- you receive two things: a liquidity event at closing AND an ongoing equity stake in the combined platform.

Here is why the equity matters: as the platform grows (more practices, more revenue, more operational efficiency), the value of your equity stake compounds. In many cases, the retained minority equity position ends up being worth far more than if the doctor had kept 100% of the practice and continued growing solo.

This is not speculative. The Thurston Group has demonstrated this pattern across 8 healthcare platforms over 38 years. "The joint venture equity partnership model we use has generated wealth for partners across many industries, with repeated success in the dental world," MODIS notes on their partnership page. "It's designed to accelerate growth and create value faster than you might on your own."

The question to ask any DSO: what is my equity worth in 3 years? In 5 years? Show me the precedent from a prior platform. If they cannot, that tells you something.

---

3. Fewer than 100 of the 1,000+ DSOs are considered qualified bidders.

There are over 1,000 invisible dental support organizations operating in the US right now. The vast majority are undercapitalized, operationally thin, and backed by first-time funds experimenting with the dental space.

Group Dentistry Now reports that "fewer than 100 of the 1,000-plus IDSOs are considered qualified bidders by industry experts." That means over 900 organizations are making offers they may not be able to fulfill.

Before you evaluate any offer, verify three things:

- Capital source: Who is the financial sponsor? How long have they been in healthcare? What is their fund size relative to their acquisition pace? (A $200M fund buying $50M of practices per year is going to need to recapitalize within 3-4 years. That recapitalization changes your deal.)
- Operator track record: How many dental platforms has the management team built before? What happened to those platforms? Can you talk to dentists who were in the previous platform?
- Staff retention: Ask for the number. If they will not give it, that tells you something. SGA's is 85%.

---

Bottom line: A DSO deal is not a number. It is a structure. The multiple is one variable in a multi-variable equation that includes EBITDA adjustments, equity terms, management fees, capital investment commitments, earn-out conditions, and the operational track record of the organization making the promise.

Get the structure right, and a partnership can be the best financial decision you have ever made. Get it wrong, and you join the 70% who end up regretting it.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Otherwise, I will be back in a few days with something most DSOs would never publish publicly.

SGA Dental Partners

emails.subject: Email 4
emails.preheader: ** We are not afraid to be measured.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

This is Mitch.

I told you last time that I would share something most DSOs would never publish. Here it is:

SGA's staff retention rate across our network is 85%.

I know that sounds like just a number. Let me explain why it is the most important metric in this entire conversation.

Your staff is your practice.

Not your equipment. Not your location. Not your patient list. Your *people*. The hygienist who has been with you for twelve years and knows Mrs. Patterson always needs the left side numbed first. The front desk coordinator who can calm an anxious patient in thirty seconds. The assistant who anticipates what you need before you ask.

Those are the people patients come back for. Those are the people who *are* the practice, whether your name is on the door or not.

And those are exactly the people who disappear when the wrong DSO takes over.

You know the story. You have heard it at conferences, on Dentaltown, in study club conversations that get quiet for a moment: "Dr. Jones is gone, there's been 2 to 3 new doctors there, all the old staff are gone."

That was a *patient* talking. Not a disgruntled dentist. A patient who walked into an office they had trusted and found strangers.

That is what happens when a DSO cuts benefits to save margin. When they replace your office manager with a "regional operations coordinator" who has never met your patients. When they eliminate the PTO policy and the CE stipend and the little things that made your team feel valued.

We do the opposite.

When a practice joins SGA, the team gets an upgrade, not a downgrade:

- Healthcare benefits through Cigna -- replacing whatever patchwork PEO plan the solo practice cobbled together
- 401(k) with Voya -- real retirement savings, not a vague promise
- Mutual of Omaha supplemental benefits -- dental, vision, life
- PTO and CE stipends -- because burning out your hygienists is how you lose them
- iSolved payroll -- replacing the manual QuickBooks nightmare your office manager has been wrestling with
- PeopleOps team -- a dedicated HR resource so your office manager is not googling "how to handle FMLA" at 9 PM

Spencer Pierce, our VP of People and Culture, described what happens during the transition: "After onboarding is completed, there's an abundance of relief. It's like 'Okay, I understand what this means for me now.'"

That relief shows up in the number: 85% retention.

Here is why I am publishing it: because it forces accountability.

If I tell you our retention rate is 85% and you join SGA and your team starts leaving, you can hold me to that number. You can call me and say, "Mitch, you told me 85%." And I will have to answer for it.

That is the point. Transparency without accountability is just marketing.

No competitor publishes this number. Not Heartland. Not Aspen. Not PDS. Not MB2. Ask them for it. Watch what happens.

Our partners know the number because they live it. Your team stays. Your patients stay. Your culture stays.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Mitch

P.S. -- I want to share one more thing in my next email. It is the story of what the first 90 days of a partnership actually look like -- step by step, no surprises. Because the fear of the unknown is often worse than any actual outcome. And I want to take that fear off the table.

emails.subject: What partnership actually looks like: the first 90 days
subjectVariants: The first 90 days after signing (no surprises)
Here is exactly what happens after you say yes
emails.preheader: ** Step by step. No mystery. No corporate jargon.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

The fear of the unknown kills more partnerships than bad economics.

Most dentists who hesitate do not hesitate because they dislike the numbers. They hesitate because they cannot picture what happens on Day 1. Day 30. Day 90. The ambiguity creates anxiety, and the anxiety creates inaction.

So here is the ambiguity, removed:

---

Pre-Close (Weeks Before Signing)

You have had your confidential conversation. You have met the partnership team. You have talked to current partners. You have brought your financial advisor into the discussion. The valuation is transparent. The equity structure is clear. You have decided this is the right move.

Before you sign, three things happen:

1. Your team is briefed. Not after the fact. Before. You decide when and how your staff hears about the partnership. We provide talking points, but you deliver the message -- because it should come from you, not from a corporate email with a logo they have never seen.

2. Nothing changes on Day 1 of the deal. Your patients' appointments are not disrupted. Your schedule does not change. Your team shows up to the same office, sees the same patients, follows the same protocols. The transition is invisible to anyone walking in the front door.

3. You get a dedicated transition coordinator. One person. Your point of contact for every question, every concern, every "wait, how does this work?" moment. Not a call center. A human being who knows your name and your practice.

---

Days 1-30: The Relief Phase

This is where the weight starts coming off.

Payroll and HR: iSolved takes over payroll processing. Your office manager stops spending two days every pay period in QuickBooks. The PeopleOps team handles benefits enrollment -- Cigna for health, Voya for 401(k), Mutual of Omaha for supplemental. Your team gets the same enrollment experience a Fortune 500 employee would get.

Accounts payable and procurement: SGA's back-office handles vendor payments, supply ordering, and insurance credentialing. The stack of invoices on your desk disappears. The supply closet gets audited (not to cut quality -- to eliminate waste and negotiate volume pricing).

Nothing clinical changes. You see the same patients. You use the same materials and labs. You set the same schedule. Nobody tells you to see more patients per hour or switch to a cheaper lab.

Spencer Pierce's observation holds: "After onboarding is completed, there's an abundance of relief."

---

Days 31-60: The Growth Phase

This is where the conversation shifts from "what am I losing?" to "what am I gaining?"

Mentor assignment: Through the Individual Growth Engine, you are paired with a clinical mentor -- another practice owner in the SGA network operating at a level you want to reach. This is not a corporate trainer. This is a peer. Someone who has been where you are and is willing to show you what they have learned.

Technology assessment: SGA evaluates your equipment and identifies investment opportunities. Digital X-ray upgrade. Intraoral scanners. Guided implant systems. CEREC. The capital comes from the platform, not from your personal line of credit.

Marketing activation: SGA's marketing team audits your digital presence. Google Business Profile optimization. Website refresh if needed. Patient acquisition campaigns tailored to your market. The marketing budget comes from the platform.

---

Days 61-90: The Connection Phase

This is where the loneliness ends.

Partner network integration: You join the partner calls. You meet the other practice owners in your region and across the network. You start hearing how Dr. Shanahan handles his lab relationships, how Dr. Godat restructured his hygiene scheduling, how the practice in Michigan solved the associate recruiting problem you have been wrestling with for two years.

4Front Symposium invitation: If the timing aligns, you attend the annual 4Front gathering. The room is full of practice owners who sound like you, think like you, and are willing to share everything they have learned. This is where the "goose bumps" happen -- where senior clinicians brainstorming together naturally elevate each other's thinking.

90-day check-in: A formal review of what has changed, what is working, and what needs adjustment. Not a performance review. A partnership review. Two-way.

---

What does not change -- at 90 days or ever:

- Your name stays on the door
- Your team stays in the chairs
- You choose the materials, the lab, the schedule
- You own equity in the combined platform for as long as you remain a partner
- Your patients see you, not a corporate replacement

---

That is it. No mystery. No corporate rebranding. No production quotas. No regional manager walking in with a clipboard.

If reading this made you think "that sounds manageable" instead of "that sounds terrifying" -- that was the point. Partnership should feel like relief, not surrender.

If you want to see how this would look specifically for your practice:

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

We will walk you through the entire process with your numbers, your team, your situation.

SGA Dental Partners

emails.subject: Email 6
emails.preheader: ** Revenue up 16.5%. Staff retention 100%. But the real number surprised everyone.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

This is Mitch. I want to introduce you to a dentist named Matt Cavendish.

Not because Matt's story is unusual. Because it is disturbingly common -- except for the ending.

Matt had been in private practice in Phoenix for twenty years. Built a solid practice. Strong patient base. Capable team. By every external measure, he was successful.

But here is how Matt described himself, in his own words, when he was honest about it:

"Maybe I was in a spot of complacency, not being challenged as much, just getting through the day and not enjoying it as much."

Twenty years. Getting through the day.

If you have been in practice for any significant stretch of time, you may recognize that feeling. It is not burnout, exactly. Burnout is dramatic. This is quieter. It is the slow erosion of the spark -- the gradual realization that the work you used to find fascinating has become routine, and the business surrounding it has become suffocating.

Matt was spending his time on things that had nothing to do with clinical excellence: "Employee benefits, wifi routers, Facebook ads -- a hundred other things that have little to do with dental excellence."

When a staff issue arose, he was on his own: "I had issues with staff that needed to be corrected and before I had a mentor, I was just trying to figure out by myself what I needed to do. It was very difficult."

Sound familiar?

Here is where Matt's story diverges from the common one.

Matt joined Gen4. We paired him with Dr. Bryan Shanahan through the Individual Growth Engine. Bryan was operating at a clinical level Matt had not yet reached. And instead of a seminar or a webinar or a binder of recommendations, Matt got something rarer: a peer who opened his practice, his lab relationships, and his treatment planning process to observation.

Matt observed his first all-on-four procedure alongside Bryan. Watched the surgical technique. Learned how Bryan worked with his lab. Picked apart the treatment planning.

"That's something I would have never had an opportunity to do if I were still in a solo practice."

Then the numbers followed:

| Metric | Result |
|--------|--------|
| Revenue (TTM) | Up 16.5% |
| EBITA vs. plan | Beating by 2.3 points |
| EBITA vs. prior year | Up $45,266 |
| Total payroll expenses | Down |
| Staff retention | 100% |
| Dental supply costs vs. plan | Beating by 6.4 points |

Those are real numbers from a real practice owned by a real dentist.

But here is the thing about Matt's story -- and the reason I am telling it to you.

Matt does not talk about the numbers first.

Matt talks about this: "When you've been in dentistry for 20 years like I have, sometimes it's not as fun as it used to be. So to be able to do a complex case like this, with somebody who is also working at a very high skill level -- it is fun."

The spark came back.

The complacency broke. The isolation ended. The peer challenge re-ignited the clinical curiosity that brought him into dentistry in the first place. And the numbers followed *because* of that -- not the other way around.

Matt offered one piece of advice for any dentist considering this: "Don't push back. There's no downside to having a mentor."

From a twenty-year practice owner who swallowed his pride and let someone else show him a better way -- that is not a small statement.

I do not know where you are in your career, Dr. {{first_name}}. Year 10. Year 20. Year 30. But I know that if you are reading this email -- if you have read all six of these emails -- something in these stories resonates.

Maybe it is the exhaustion. Maybe it is the isolation. Maybe it is the complacency you have not admitted to anyone yet. Or maybe it is simpler than all of that: you miss being excited about the work.

Whatever it is, you are not alone. And you do not have to figure it out by yourself.

Schedule a Confidential Conversation

Mitch

P.S. -- I have one more email for you. It is short, and it is different. Instead of me talking, I am going to ask you one question. Your answer will help me understand where you are -- and what would be most useful to you next.

subjectVariants: Which of these describes where you are right now?
One question. Three options. Your answer shapes what comes next.
emails.preheader: ** No essay. No phone call. Just one click.
emails.body:
Dr. {{first_name}},

I promised this would be short. And different.

Over the past four weeks, I have shared our values, our valuation insights, our staff retention rate, our first-90-days process, and Dr. Cavendish's transformation story. That is a lot of reading, and I appreciate your time.

Now I want to ask one question. Not to sell you anything. To understand where you are so I can send you the right thing next.

Which of these best describes your situation right now?

---

A) I am exploring. I am researching options and educating myself. I am not ready to talk to anyone yet, and I may not be for a while. Keep sending me useful content -- market intelligence, case studies, industry trends. I will reach out when I am ready.

[Click here if this is you]

---

B) I am comparing. I have talked to at least one DSO or broker. I am actively evaluating options and trying to figure out which model is right for my practice. I would like to see SGA's specific approach compared to what I have already heard.

[Click here if this is you]

---

C) I am ready to talk. I have done my research. I know what I am looking for. I want a confidential conversation with someone who can answer my specific questions about my specific practice.

[Click here if this is you]

---

That is it. One click. No form. No phone call.

Your answer determines what I send you next -- and equally important, what I do *not* send you. I have no interest in overwhelming your inbox with content that does not match where you are.

Whichever you choose, know this: there is no wrong answer and no expiration date. The conversation is here whenever you are ready for it.

Thank you for reading, Dr. {{first_name}}.

Mitch Ellingson, DDS
Chief Clinical Officer
SGA Dental Partners

P.S. -- If you have a colleague who would find these emails useful -- a fellow practice owner who is quietly researching their options at 11 PM like most of us did -- feel free to forward this. They can sign up here: [link to lead magnet]. We will start them at the beginning.
